**Articles | April 1, 2024**

# Spotlight on Gene Therapies in Q2 2024 Trends

Our latest short quarterly insight on healthcare news for plan sponsors focuses on the growing gene therapy pipeline.

It covers:

#### Get the Publication

![Spotlight on Gene Therapies in Q2 2024 Trends](/media/3686/lab-technicians.jpg?crop=0.287056873762076,0,0,0&cropmode=percentage&width=664&height=416&rnd=133564622450000000 "Spotlight on Gene Therapies in Q2 2024 Trends")

Share this page

This issue of *Trends* also includes a reminder that draft guidance on Part D plans for RDS and creditable coverage has implications for retiree health benefits.

## Key statistics

Gene therapy is a novel approach to treat, cure or ultimately prevent disease by changing the expression of a person’s genes. In recent years, there’s been dramatic growth in the number of gene therapy trials.

### Number of Trials for Gene Therapies by Start Year

![This bar graph shows how the number of trials for gene therapies has grown since 2000, from 19 that year to 117 in 2023, which was a dramatic increase from 17 in 2022.](/media/3688/g1_gene-therapies-trial.svg)

Source: Segal using information about gene therapy trials on ClinicalTrials.gov

## The growing gene therapy pipeline

The gene therapy pipeline is dynamic and continues to expand with a focus on addressing a wide range of diseases and conditions. 2023 was a breakthrough year, with seven gene therapies approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). As of March 18, 2024, there are now 36 gene therapies [approved by the FDA](https://www.fda.gov/vaccines-blood-biologics/cellular-gene-therapy-products/approved-cellular-and-gene-therapy-products), with an additional 500 in the pipeline and the expectation that 10–20 will be approved annually by 2025.

Cancer is the area that holds most promise, with the potential for FDA approval of the first Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell therapy (CART-T) for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. This approval would impact a large patient population. Furthermore, research is underway for gene therapy for osteoarthritis, a form of arthritis that affects over [32.5 million](https://www.cdc.gov/arthritis/types/osteoarthritis.htm?CDC_AA_refVal=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cdc.gov%2Farthritis%2Fbasics%2Fosteoarthritis.htm) U.S. adults.

The growth of gene therapy has put pressure on plan sponsors to review strategies for their covered population, including financial implications and decisions about coverage criteria.

### What is gene therapy and how much does it cost?

In general, gene therapy involves replacing a gene that causes a medical problem with one that does not, adding genes to help the body fight or treat disease, or turning off genes that cause medical problems. The administration of gene therapies is complex, often involving extended hospital stays, supplementary services and medications.

The total cost of gene therapy comes with a high price. While the number of people needing these therapies is currently small, the costs are extremely high. For example, a one-time injection of Hemgenix® for treatment of adults with hemophilia B costs $3.5 million. In December 2023, two new therapies to treat sickle cell disease were approved, Casgevy™ and Lyfgenia™, with treatment costing $2.2–3.1 million. These price points make gene therapies unaffordable for many plan sponsors.

## Compliance reminder

### Implications of draft guidance on Part D plans for RDS and creditable coverage

Plan sponsors that offer retiree health benefits need to take a close look at that coverage in 2024 to ensure that it reflects new draft guidance from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services that may affect creditability determinations in 2025. Plan sponsors also may want to consider whether a Medicare Advantage program might be the right solution for their retirees. Read more in our February 20, 2024 [insight](/consulting-insights/cms-draft-guidance-part-d-plans-rds-creditable-coverage).

enriched_points.expanded_content: A significant 68.5% of current gene therapy trials focus on cancer, highlighting the urgent need for innovative treatments in oncology and the potential for breakthroughs.
Many trials are increasingly aimed at rare genetic disorders, addressing unmet needs and leveraging gene therapy's potential to offer curative solutions for these conditions.
Beyond cancer, gene therapies are expanding into non-oncology areas, including cardiovascular diseases and neurological disorders, showcasing versatility in therapeutic applications.
Ex-vivo gene therapy methods are gaining traction, allowing for targeted modifications of patients' cells outside the body before reintroduction, enhancing treatment efficacy.
In-vivo gene therapy techniques are evolving rapidly, enabling direct delivery of therapeutic genes to target tissues, thus minimizing invasive procedures and improving patient outcomes.
